speaker: interviewer
text: January 28, 1996 with Mrs. Sonia Hochman. And I'm Marianne Kador. So you walked out of the grotto. And you are now on the field--

speaker: interviewee
text: We're running.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're running.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're running.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we go further, I just wanted to ask you, how did you spend this time, this five, six months in the grotto as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, we-- I used to-- my grandmother used to teach us poetry. They taught us Tanakh. That's the Hebrew history, you know, the, the Bible. Whatever they remembered orally, they would teach us.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were about nine, 10?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because in the dark-- yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know how to read and write?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, because I just went-- I, I, I didn't know Hebrew yet. But I-- but my, my mother would sit and tell us the stories. We were told about King David, about King Shaul, you know, and about the, the Shofetim-- that with the prophets, and-- because they had to keep us occupied. And they taught us poetry. And what happened was that I would study the poem. And my sister was so smart that she knew before me, my little sister, Sima. And we would sit and sing. And you know, the children, we were the entertainment committee. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, we were running. We came up to Mrs. [NON-ENGLISH]. We came to her home. She had a barn. My aunt brought us up to the barn. In the morning, she went down.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which town was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in Bilcze.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. I really don't know. I will look it up for you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this near, near Korolowka?

speaker: interviewee
text: Near Korolowka, a few miles away from Korolowka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Small town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a village, a big village.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they, they didn't know your family and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, my grandmother was born there. And my grandfather, he used to handle, he used to deal, do business with all these people there. Anyway, this woman says, that yes, yesterday that she, she knows the people-- my, my aunt told them what happened. She says, yes, in fact, the Germans gave them to the Ukrainian police. And they're holding them in jail and that the Ukrainian police is going back. They have to-- they're trying, you know, to find out where they are, to bring out their possessions, and to find the other people. Well, when we heard Ukrainian police have them, we felt, well, there's still a chance.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was nobody else left in the grotto?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we all escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: While in the grotto, did-- you were all religious people. Did you observe--

speaker: interviewee
text: No--

speaker: interviewer
text: --whatever you could?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any praying going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Praying, yes. Praying, always. But we didn't have-- first of all, we didn't eat no ham or anything like that. What did we eat? We had a potato. And what did we eat?

speaker: interviewer
text: We made a pletzel. You know what a pletzel is? Like it's, like it's, like a little, I would say, like a bialy, like a bialy. We would bring a little bit flour with water. And they-- and what they bake? The whole thing that would make us from a few potatoes and a little bit bialy, they would make a soup. What did we have? What did we have? What was there to be kosher with?

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, we heard that they're-- by-- so my aunts were already very optimistic. And I'm crying. And Sal, my cousin [NON-ENGLISH]-- his name now is Sal-- Sal is crying, because his mother and brother were caught and my mother and sister were caught. Anyway, the Jewish doctor, [NON-ENGLISH] was very-- he was friendly with the Ukrainian captain of the police. My father and my grandfather and my uncle came running to him, actually. And they started to beg him, he should go and intervene and tell them they caught him my cousin [NON-ENGLISH], which was my grandfather's nephew. He was extremely well-to-do. He had-- Jewish people didn't have a complete falvarak. That's a complete estate, where hundreds of acres of, of land, you know, that peasants were working on. He had something like this. He owned-- he was a-- he had this before the war. He had a lot of gold and diamonds and American dollars and Russian gold pieces. And he said he will dig up everything. He'll give it to the Ukrainian police. They should let them go, the five people go, the man and two women and two small children. The policemen weren't so anxious. But then they said, fine, but you have to dig graves and bring bodies to put in in place of these people. Whatever bodies are going to be in the graves, those are the people we won't shoot.

speaker: interviewer
text: So look at this terrible-- but they had to go. They had to go to towns to search, where they just had an Aktion. Because this is-- it's, it's April. The ground is still very, very, very hard from, from the frost, you know, from the winter. And they had to go to, to cemeteries, to Jewish cemeteries, where they had Aktions, where they killed Jews, to search for people, to bring a man and two women and two small children.

speaker: interviewee
text: They found a man and two women. But they couldn't find any children. And my father says, no, he's going to try. He's going to go to another town. He has to save Simala. He has to save his child.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my grandfather went with him. Anyway, they were caught by the Ukrainian police. Another Ukrainian police, they want to know why they were digging up graves. They had to tell them. They took them to jail. They beat them up. Anyway, what happened was a day elapsed. And the captain of the police was waiting. He kept going to the Dr. [NON-ENGLISH]. He said, where are they? He wanted to do it the night before, but they didn't have any baby, the children, the boy and a girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, the following day, a, a Ukrainian woman came to the police to ask, beg, that they should let my Aunt [NON-ENGLISH] and her son go. That night, my father and my grandfather brought the bodies. They found two children. And they dug the graves. And they put the bodies in the graves. And they were sitting on the side of the cemetery. The captain from the police took my mother and my sister, [NON-ENGLISH] and my Aunt [NON-ENGLISH] to the cemetery. He told them to lay down, not to be scared and that he's only shooting in the air. And as soon as he shoots, they should run away. Well, shot in the air. My mother got up. And she saw that she was alive, and she saw her child is alive. My cousin got up, [NON-ENGLISH], and he takes a look. My Aunt [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] are dead. They're not moving. My mother started to cry because she loved her. This was my grandmother's sister. And she was the dearest aunt. She lived with us. And she loved her like a sister. She started to cry. The Ukrainian policeman said, if you're not going to run, I can do the same for you. So she grabbed my sister, Sima. And she ran. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you imagine? They had to leave her. But what could they do? They ran.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, who was there with my grandfather with a horse and wagon, they took them away. And they came back to our town. That night, my Uncle Nisel also came, and he brought us home, me, Chana, Jetta, and Schloime. We came back to my grandmother's house. And I saw my mother, and I saw my sister. God, how happy I was. How proud I was. I couldn't believe it. I couldn't-- a miracle happened. She's still alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was hugging them and kissing them. [SOBBING] God, can you imagine how sad my cousin Sal felt? His mother and brother weren't there. They were killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: To this day, he suffers. And to this day, I feel guilty somehow that I was fortunate to have a mother and a sister, and he lost his. Things are getting progressively worse in our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Jews left there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, those men still with their shields were still free.

speaker: interviewer
text: They could travel still?

speaker: interviewee
text: They still could travel at that point. But the head of the police comes and he tells them that they don't think that they're safe. Because he was friendly with my father, my father says, let's go to-- he takes us to a peasant's house. We had given, all of us gave possessions to our favorite peasant, people.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is still in 1943?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is '43, April. April '43. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's before Passover, a week before Passover. We go to, to my father's friend. And he lets us lay in the barn. My grandmother and grandfather and their children ran to another peasant. And we're afraid to stay in this, in the bunker in the attic, since Ukrainian police know that we're-- know that we came back from the grotto. They know because they had to pay off these policemen, too. They say they're going to come into the building. They'll tear apart the building, and they'll all, they'll find us. We have to run. So we go. My father brings us into the barn. And there's some straw there. We had no-- my mother had no clothing left. I think she didn't even have a blanket. But we laid down. My father figured he'll go and he'll start-- secure some food for us and clothing for us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And at night it started to rain. Oh, my god, April in the Ukraine, the rains are so harsh, stormy. And what do you have here? The roof is made out of straw. And the rain is coming down at you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother is laying on top of me and of my sister, Sima, like you see a, a cat with kittens trying to protect-- or a dog with puppies. She's laying on top of us that we shouldn't get wet. And she is drenched, wet. The water was running from her.

speaker: interviewer
text: A few days later, well, what can we do? In fact, she dried herself. We were laying wet. But we were alive. So you don't complain. My sister sees-- there was, there was like a little-- you could see through a crack on the, on the-- to see what was going on. And, oh my god, what is it? You know, in the rest of the, of the outside of the barn, she saw chickens walking around. And she says, Mommy, why are these chickens allowed to walk around, and I'm not? What could my mother say to a child? She said, Mommy, I'd like to walk around outside, too. My mother said to her, shh, quiet. Shh, that's all we children knew was shh, don't talk. Don't talk.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were quiet. We were so trained that you couldn't believe-- today I can't believe it myself, [SOBBING] how my sister and I, when we had to make-- excuse me-- we would hold it in for a night. Of if not-- but we wouldn't even cry when we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: We were wet. We were hungry. We were starved. What could we do?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was the worst thing to be a child during that time. Nobody wanted to be with children. Nobody wanted to have children around. There were very, very bad times. They were times--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were dangerous to the community.

speaker: interviewee
text: People used to abandon children, a lot of people. They would go into hiding. And unfortunately, a lot of children were abandoned. I don't say a lot. But some children were abandoned. And I heard-- used to hear these stories. They would say that these people went into a bunker. But they would take in the parents. But the people that were already in the bunker said, you can come in but not your children.

speaker: interviewer
text: What could the parents do? Anyway, these were, these were times that should never-- in any century in any time, should ever be times like this, again. Never. Did you hear this as a child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. And I, and I knew I had to be quiet. I, I wasn't allowed to-- I was so brave, and I was so, I was so fast, because I was afraid. Not-- my parents wouldn't have done that. But I heard what happened to other children. So I was scared. I was frightened. Anyway, at night, my father came. I think he went-- he asked this peasant if he could give my mother back some clothing. And he, he didn't. So my father says, I don't think you're safe here. Come. Let's go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did we go? We want to the woods. We went to the woods. There were some other families in the woods. It was Passover time.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to the woods. We were laying in the woods. Oh, my god, and I'm so scared. But I still have a mother. I still have a father. I still have a sister. My grandmother is alive, the whole family. And I feel I'm so lucky. But I'm frightened. At night, you would hear the dogs bark from the farmers. And we used to think that the police was coming for us. And we would try to hide, you know, under leaves, under, oh god-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we hear a man comes running. And he says that in Borshchiv is a, a very big Aktion. And in that Aktion, they took away my Uncle Leib, my cousin Chaim, and my entire Kavolik family. They discovered a very big bunker. It was the first day or second day of, of Passover. And they shot them. And the children, they threw in alive. And the graves were so high that the peasants said that the bodies were rising. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, people are walking around. They're saying, [NON-ENGLISH]. Where do we go? What do we do? The forest is not safe. We have no place where to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: My little sister, Sima, is four years old at that point. She's walking around just like the grown-ups and mimicking, [NON-ENGLISH]. We became like-- we children mimic the grown ups. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, we, we are so desperate. The men already are told not to come back, to watch out, that the people with the shields are not safe anymore. And they, they are starting to make-- Borshchiv is going to become Judenfrei. And they are, they are-- the, the Aktions are getting closer and closer. And they're, they're cleansing. They're making-- this is 1943. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My Uncle Nisel went to a forester. This forester-- my Uncle Schloime also went with him. This forester had-- was a watchman for-- this forest belonged to a Jewish man. This forester was hiding a Jewish-- Mr. [NON-ENGLISH] owned this forest. And he had promised him this forest. He-- in fact, he signed it over to him that if he saves his grandson, the forest would belong to him. My Uncle Nisel was friends with him. And he came and he said to him, listen, I'm looking for a place to hide my family. He says, look, there is another grotto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not too far from here, there is a grotto. I know. It's on the land that belongs to the priests. They called it Popowa Yama. That the-- it belongs to the priests. And it was like a ditch. A yama is a ditch. He says, I think there is a grotto there. So he and my Uncle Schloime and, and the grandson of Mr. [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] Dodek and Mendel Dodek took a lot of rope. And they went to look where this grotto is. In order to get into this grotto, you had to go with your feet first and with your hand up. And you had to slide in. There was mud. It was at-- it was-- a hill was, like, was surrounding it. And there was a big ditch. And that you had to slide in. They slid in. They came in. They had to crawl. Anyway, they came in. And they found a place. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In fact, they were walking and walking. They couldn't find an end. And they found water in that grotto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it dark there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dark, pitch black. You don't see nothing in high places, in low places. And they came back. And they said, they found a, a home for us, a haven for us. 39 people was the Dodek family, the Stermer family, the [NON-ENGLISH] family. And [NON-ENGLISH] came in with us. And the two brothers of the [NON-ENGLISH], my father's two cousins who ran away when they had the big Aktion, you know. They were not killed with the rest of the family. They came with us. And we gathered on the hill. And we had to start to go into this grotto. It wasn't so easy. You had to slide down in the mud. And then there were people standing there. They were, like, maneuvering you to get you in. Came to my grandmother, she was a tall woman, big-boned. She had a little-- she had a problem. She got stuck. Anyway, they pushed her through. We came into the grotto. And, and we started to look to make a place for ourselves, to make a life for ourselves. We lived underground. In that grotto, we gathered May the 5th, 1943. And we lived in this grotto till April 12, 1944. We were liberated by the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father still able to come and go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but at night. No more legal. No more legal. My father-- each one of us that went into this grotto had friends. These friends were righteous Christians. And in fact, my uncles went back. And they, and they were honored two years ago. They made a big memorial there for this big Aktion was Passover time in Borshchiv. And they-- in Borshchiv they had a big-- they honored all these righteous Christians, because--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were Polish farmers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish and Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ukrainian farmers.

speaker: interviewee
text: But these people we should never forget. There were people that did do us good. Without this people we could never have survived. You know, there were-- most of the people were bad. But there were some good also. Anyway, we came into this grotto. We started-- the men brought in boards and wood, and-- [SIGHS] we came in in May, which was the beginning of the summer. And we brought in some provisions with us. And we were very lucky. We were not far away from fields. So at night when they had potatoes and they had corn in the fields-- And I have to tell you the ingenuity of the men. They bought from the peasants two stones. And they made a mill, so we could make our own flour. We brought in two-- from iron made like a little fondues, you know. And they would bring in wood and cut wood. And they, they made from wood a gun. So the peasants would think we, that they have guns. They would go out at night. And from far away, if the peasant was in the field, they would carry something on their hands. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were very, very brave, because we were underneath. And we were safe. And they had to go out to bring us provisions, food. One time, my father was on the outside. He used to have this friend. This friend of his was hiding a Jewish boy. I don't remember the boy's name. I knew it. I forgot it. And he's now in Israel. He survived. He was in Aryan papers. This boy used to help my father bring the provisions to the grotto, to the entrance. And the men started to look for a second exit, because we were already afraid. They already-- as soon as we settled, they started to look for another exit in case they discovered this exit Well, we knew one thing. If they come in, they have to come in first with feet. And we had axes. The men would stand with axes. And in fact, when the men would leave and when they would come in, they had to give a password. And every time the password used to change, because we were afraid in case somebody is caught and they shouldn't bring back the Ukrainian police with them or the Gestapo. Well, my father was on the outside--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were given axes in case the men come down--

speaker: interviewee
text: To chop off their feet. They brought in--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were, you were ready for that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were ready. You see, what happened was we had from our home still-- we came from our house, from my grandmother's house. So there was still axes and shovels and nails and things. We brought everything, whatever we could put our hands on, because we lived already in one grotto, so we knew already what to expect in the other grotto. What happened was my father was on the outside to get together provisions. And he would stay, like, a week or two with this man. This man was a wonderful friend of his. And he would go around and gather and get him oil and flour and whatever he could. And of course, my father would pay him dearly for everything, with gold and with American dollars. My father comes with this boy, to, to come back into the cave, to the grotto. Takes a look. What happened was the peasant found out. Somebody saw somebody go in. They went and told the police. And the police came with peasants. And they threw in rocks and dirt. And they covered up the entrance. This is in the middle of the night. My father runs back to this man, to his friend. And he gets another few friends, people that he could trust. They come with shovels. He had $20 left over on him, American dollars. He gave them. And he opens up the entrance. He digs out the entrance. And he comes in. And we're sleeping. All of a sudden we see a reflector, a light. Oh, my god, we're discovered. We're frightened. We hear my father say, don't worry, don't worry, it's me. Come, all of you. Get dressed. Let's get out. The police know about you. The Gestapo will find out. They'll come in, or they'll keep watch, and we'll all starve inside. Get dressed, and let's go.

speaker: interviewer
text: My grandmother, again, says, no, we're not going no place. Who said that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your grandmother.

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandmother. And my uncles--

speaker: interviewer
text: She was a formidable woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was the leader. She was the leader. She was the most courageous lady I have ever come across. My grandmother Ester Stermer, in fact, she wrote a book, by Ester Stermer. I will show you later the pictures of Ester Stermer, the most wonderful lady in the whole world, my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you went to bed at night, you're still in the grotto--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sleeping next to my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And you closed your eyes. What kind of fantasies you had?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fantasies that I'll survive. And I'll be able to live. And I'll be able to dance and sing and walk outside and go to school like the rest of the children, and how fortunate it is that I'm sleeping next to my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: You felt fortunate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I felt very fortunate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because the family was together--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because-- yes, my friends were not so fortunate. All of them were killed and their families.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you knew that already when you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, yes. Anyway, my father came in. And we tell him here is money, go out, and tell them that we ran away. Close up the entrance. What happened was while my father was away, my uncles dug out another exit. We had already another exit. They made up with my uncles where to meet the following night in the middle of the field. And then he'll come in. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were so fortunate. We were safe underneath. But the men were very, very-- a few times-- the police were watching us. A few times, they were in the fields, and they were shooting after them. The men would go out and bring in-- one night when it was stormy, when it was raining, my grandmother would figure out when, when the moon was not so high, they would go out to the forest, and they would bring in, they would cut up woods. They would go-- when the, the peasants were digging potatoes, they would steal potatoes. One night they would bring potatoes. All night, they would carry potatoes. And then the corn, then wheat, because we had our own mill. This is unbelievable what these men did. Anyway, once they found out that we're there, everybody was afraid to be the first one to get out of the exit. So my grandmother had another idea. She made an effigy. She made a man. She dressed up a man, like a scarecrow. She dressed up a man in a, in a suit with a hat and a stick. And they would push out that man. And when they saw that nobody was shooting at them, the men would go out. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Anyway, the only time we would sit and breathe, afraid to breathe, is when the men would be out, because it was very dangerous for them. We never knew if they'll come back or not. When they left, we never knew if they'll come back or not. This is already, I would say, I am now January or February 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you hear about-- the war was going on. Did they bring in--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is what I'm telling.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We come back. And they say the front isn't too far, not too far. We're excited. Anyway, a few weeks later, the men go out, and they find out that we were liberated. Can you imagine?

speaker: interviewer
text: The front went through us. We did not hear anything. We're underneath. Can you imagine? They come back. The Russians were here, and they left. This is March, beginning of March.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, all my uncles went to a friend-- I think my father went to this friend that had the Jewish boy. And they made up with him that he would let in a big, a long rope with a bottle. And in that bottle it would say that we were liberated. Anyway, we know that a front is around us, because what was happening-- the Germans were fighting forward and retreating, you know. And the-- the front was going on for another month in our vicinity.

speaker: interviewer
text: One day they go and they find the bottle. And we were liberated. Can you imagine how we felt? We lived to see the destruction of Hitler.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We survived. [SOBBING] God, how we used to pray. All we wanted was just to see-- we didn't even care if we lived or not, but Hitler should, should not be around. But his army should perish. And he-- and his regime should perish. This was everybody's prayer. The men went out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] For a week they-- we still stayed in the grotto because we were afraid the front shouldn't go back and forth. A week later, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they came with trucks, with Russian soldiers, trucks. And they took us out from the grotto. We came out--

speaker: interviewer
text: We continue in a minute with this story.
